customer_summary.summary: This task initiates the migration of the SASS compiler used in the Angular build pipeline from the legacy `sass` (node-sass) or `sass-loader`-based tooling to the new Angular-native builder-based compiler integrated via `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application`. This change is required as part of the Angular 19 upgrade, which deprecates older SASS compilation strategies. Without this migration, the project will either fail to build after Angular 19 is applied or may produce incorrect styles due to incompatibilities with modern Angular CLI tooling.

developer_context.big_picture: This project is the UVZ frontend application — a large Angular-based enterprise UI built for internal government use, currently on Angular 18 LTS and migrating to Angular 19. It uses a modular architecture with shared UI components, adapters for backend integration, and relies heavily on SASS for styling. The migration to the new Angular SASS compiler is needed because Angular 19 drops support for the older `sass`/`node-sass` stack and enforces use of `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application`, which bundles a modern Dart-based SASS compiler and stricter import resolution. If not done, the build will break, and styling regressions (e.g., broken themes, missing variables, failed `@use`/`@import` resolution) may silently occur — risking visual or functional regressions in production.

in: Update Angular build configuration (`angular.json`) to use `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application` instead of legacy builders
Verify SASS import compatibility (e.g., `@use` vs `@import`, variable scoping, `_variables.scss` resolution)
Run end-to-end and visual regression tests to detect style-related regressions
Ensure build tooling (Webpack, Karma, Playwright) remains compatible with the new build pipeline
out: Modifying application logic or component behavior (only styles and build configuration are in scope)
Upgrading Angular version itself (assumed already handled in the broader Angular 19 migration effort)
Refactoring SASS source code unless strictly required for compatibility with the new compiler

context_boundaries.boundary: Angular 19 enforces use of `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application` and removes legacy `sass`/`node-sass` support — the existing SASS setup using `sass-loader` or `angular-cli`-style SASS preprocessing will no longer work without migration.

Older SASS tooling (e.g., `sass-loader`, `sass`) may conflict with Angular 19’s bundled Dart SASS compiler, leading to duplicate or incompatible SASS processing. This risks build-time errors or inconsistent style resolution.

Custom SASS imports (e.g., relative paths, `~`, or absolute paths via global styles) may break if the new compiler enforces stricter module resolution or disallows legacy `@import`-based global variable injection.
source_facts: Global styles entrypoint (inferred from Angular project structure)

context_boundaries.boundary: Even minor SASS behavior changes (e.g., variable shadowing, `!default` handling, or `@use` vs `@import` semantics) can cause visual regressions undetectable by unit tests — full E2E and visual diff testing is required.

developer_context.architecture_notes: This migration sits at the *infrastructure layer* of the frontend build pipeline — specifically, it touches the *build configuration boundary* (build system → builder selection) and indirectly the *styling layer* (SASS files). The application uses a modular architecture with shared SASS variables, components, and modules. The SASS compilation is triggered via the Angular CLI builder during `ng build`/`ng serve`. The new `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application` builder replaces the older `browser` builder and now embeds Dart SASS as its default compiler. Neighboring components include: (1) Custom Webpack configuration — if SASS preprocessing is configured outside Angular CLI, it may need cleanup; (2) Test runner configuration — if SASS preprocessing is used for tests (e.g., `stylePreprocessorOptions`), it must be aligned; (3) Global styles entrypoint — likely where global SASS variables are imported and may need migration to `@use`-style imports if needed. The developer’s task is to ensure the *transition from legacy to new builder* is seamless, without breaking existing styling behavior.

anticipated_questions.question: Do I need to change all `@import` statements to `@use` in my SASS files?
anticipated_questions.answer: Not necessarily — Angular’s new compiler still supports `@import` for backwards compatibility, but it's strongly recommended to migrate to `@use` for better modularity and future-proofing. Only change if you encounter scoping issues or warnings during build.

anticipated_questions.question: Will this affect local development (e.g., `ng serve`) or only production builds?
anticipated_questions.answer: Both — the builder change affects *all* Angular CLI commands (`build`, `serve`, `test`), so local development will use the new compiler as well. Ensure your local dev environment matches CI.

anticipated_questions.question: What if my SASS files use `~` (tilde) imports like `@import '~styles/variables';`?
anticipated_questions.answer: The new Angular builder no longer resolves `~` to `node_modules` by default. You must either migrate to absolute paths under `src/` or configure `stylePreprocessorOptions.includePaths` in `angular.json` explicitly.

anticipated_questions.question: Is this tied to the Angular 19 upgrade, or can we do it separately?
anticipated_questions.answer: This is tightly coupled — Angular 19 removes support for the legacy `sass`/`node-sass` stack. Delaying this migration risks build failure once Angular 19 is applied.
